revision.text:
[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''神幸祭'''（しんこうさい）は、神霊の御幸{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}}（ぎょこう、みゆき）が行われる[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の祭礼。'''神幸式'''（しんこうしき）とも言う。多くの場合、神霊が宿った[[Shintai|神体]]や[[Yorishiro|依り代]]などを[[Mikoshi|神輿]]に移して、[[Ujigami|氏子]]地域内に御幸したり、[[Otabisho|御旅所]]や元宮に渡御したりする。神輿や鳳輦の登場する祭礼のほとんどは、神幸祭の一種といえる。 In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == 概要 == 神幸祭の一般的な意義は産土神が氏子の土地を巡る事で神威を示し、神と氏子が親しく交歓する機会にある{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。神事が混交している場合もあるが、神幸祭の形式は神の出自や性格によって次の3つに大別できる{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 ; ミアレ型 : 神が坐す・神が降りる御生（ミアレ）の地から、神威の再生のために神を社に迎える形式。例として[[Aoi Matsuri|葵祭]]が挙げられる。 ; ミソギ型 : 怨霊・疫神などの祟り神を水辺や海の禊ぎ場に送り、氏子や神具とともに禊祓する形式。[[Gion Matsuri|祇園祭]]の本来の姿である御霊会は禊ぎのための神幸である。今日では少なくなった形式だが宇出津町の[[Abare Festival|あばれ祭り]]などに見られる。 ; オイデ型 : 氏地内に御旅所を設け、神との交歓のために御出（オイデ）を乞う形式。[[Matsunoo Taisha|松尾大社]]の松尾祭などが例として挙げられるが、産土神の神幸祭は一般的にオイデ型である。 ギズム型 三陸町綾里、吉浜に見られる、神輿渡御の際に、見られる現象。神輿が不可思議な動きをする事をギズム神輿と言います。また、これが起きれば、塩蒔き、権現様が静めようとする。またこの現象は、神の意志による、考え。(綾里地区の人の話)吉浜の根白地区の総鎮守、市杵嶋姫神社は、荒神輿であります。 また、三陸町吉浜では、[[Mikoshi|御神輿]]が暴れると言います。各神社の神輿には、鈴が糸に４個(前後ろ会わせて)付いており、巡航の際｢ジャラジャラ｣と音を立てて激しく回る。 {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 本来は、神霊を集落内の[[Altar|祭壇]]に迎えたものが、祭壇が神社に発展すると、迎える行為が逆の意味の「里帰り」として残り、神幸祭になったと言われている。このため、磐座などの降臨の地が御旅所となり、現在では元宮や元の鎮座地である場合が多い{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 御旅所に向う神幸祭の流れは、 # 神輿などに神霊を移す神事 # 神社から御旅所への渡御 # 御旅所での神事や奉納（御旅所祭） # 御旅所から神社への還御 # 神霊を還す神事 In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. 神輿を巡幸のために神社の境内から出すことを'''宮出し'''（みやだし）、巡幸を終えた神輿が境内に戻ることを'''宮入り'''（みやいり）という。この語は、稚児や[[Ujigami|氏子]]から奉納される山車などが神社から出る・入る場合にも用いられる。 == 渡御行列 == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] 渡御は多くの氏子が祭礼に関わることの出来る場面の一つで、神輿の担ぎ手になったり、祭礼の規模の大きなところでは山車、[[Lion dance|獅子舞]]や舞踊などを繰り出し行列になる。渡御行列の順序は祭礼によってまちまちであるが、行列の先頭部には先導役の[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|猿田彦]]がいることが多く、その次に[[Gohei|御幣]]、笠鉾、獅子、[[Nobori|幟]]、高張り提灯、[[Drum|太鼓]]などの露払いの役目を持つものや先頭を示すものがあって、神輿がくることが多い{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。このほかには、[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Miko|巫女]]、稚児、山車、獅子、舞踏などが行列に含まれる。渡御の道中は音楽的にも多様で、神職による[[Gagaku|雅楽]]、氏子による祭囃子や[[Ondō|音頭]]などが奏でられることが多い。また、行列の際には、三陸町綾里、同三陸町吉浜では、御神輿が、急に、不可解な動きをする事をギジム神輿と言う。三陸町吉浜根白地区の総鎮守は、荒神輿と言われている。また、ギジムの際になれば、権現様、塩祓いがその動きを懸命に静めようとする。また、前や、後ろに[[Mikoshi|御神輿]]が行ったりすることをギズム神輿(なお、これは神の意志による、考えのよう。)また、巡航の際、不可思議な動きをする事があり、これはギズム神輿と呼ばれている。●また、神輿の担ぎ手は毎年１６人を出し、いない場合その人の親戚から出す。また、綾里地区では、陸尺と呼ばれている。 The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) 夜間の渡御では、提灯行列が加わったり、山車に明りが灯されるなどする。 == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''神幸祭'''（しんこうさい）は、神霊の御幸{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}}（ぎょこう、みゆき）が行われる[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の祭礼。'''神幸式'''（しんこうしき）とも言う。多くの場合、神霊が宿った[[Shintai|神体]]や[[Yorishiro|依り代]]などを[[Mikoshi|神輿]]に移して、[[Ujigami|氏子]]地域内に御幸したり、[[Otabisho|御旅所]]や元宮に渡御したりする。神輿や鳳輦の登場する祭礼のほとんどは、神幸祭の一種といえる。 In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == 概要 == 神幸祭の一般的な意義は産土神が氏子の土地を巡る事で神威を示し、神と氏子が親しく交歓する機会にある{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。神事が混交している場合もあるが、神幸祭の形式は神の出自や性格によって次の3つに大別できる{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 ; Miare type A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. ; misogi type : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. Oide type This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. Gism type A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[Mikoshi|mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 本来は、神霊を集落内の[[Altar|祭壇]]に迎えたものが、祭壇が神社に発展すると、迎える行為が逆の意味の「里帰り」として残り、神幸祭になったと言われている。このため、磐座などの降臨の地が御旅所となり、現在では元宮や元の鎮座地である場合が多い{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 御旅所に向う神幸祭の流れは、 # 神輿などに神霊を移す神事 # 神社から御旅所への渡御 # 御旅所での神事や奉納（御旅所祭） # 御旅所から神社への還御 # 神霊を還す神事 In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. 神輿を巡幸のために神社の境内から出すことを'''宮出し'''（みやだし）、巡幸を終えた神輿が境内に戻ることを'''宮入り'''（みやいり）という。この語は、稚児や[[Ujigami|氏子]]から奉納される山車などが神社から出る・入る場合にも用いられる。 == 渡御行列 == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] 渡御は多くの氏子が祭礼に関わることの出来る場面の一つで、神輿の担ぎ手になったり、祭礼の規模の大きなところでは山車、[[Lion dance|獅子舞]]や舞踊などを繰り出し行列になる。渡御行列の順序は祭礼によってまちまちであるが、行列の先頭部には先導役の[[Sarutahiko Ōkami|猿田彦]]がいることが多く、その次に[[Gohei|御幣]]、笠鉾、獅子、[[Nobori|幟]]、高張り提灯、[[Drum|太鼓]]などの露払いの役目を持つものや先頭を示すものがあって、神輿がくることが多い{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。このほかには、[[Kannushi|神職]]、[[Miko|巫女]]、稚児、山車、獅子、舞踏などが行列に含まれる。渡御の道中は音楽的にも多様で、神職による[[Gagaku|雅楽]]、氏子による祭囃子や[[Ondō|音頭]]などが奏でられることが多い。また、行列の際には、三陸町綾里、同三陸町吉浜では、御神輿が、急に、不可解な動きをする事をギジム神輿と言う。三陸町吉浜根白地区の総鎮守は、荒神輿と言われている。また、ギジムの際になれば、権現様、塩祓いがその動きを懸命に静めようとする。また、前や、後ろに[[Mikoshi|御神輿]]が行ったりすることをギズム神輿(なお、これは神の意志による、考えのよう。)また、巡航の際、不可思議な動きをする事があり、これはギズム神輿と呼ばれている。●また、神輿の担ぎ手は毎年１６人を出し、いない場合その人の親戚から出す。また、綾里地区では、陸尺と呼ばれている。 The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. 神輿を巡幸のために神社の境内から出すことを'''宮出し'''（みやだし）、巡幸を終えた神輿が境内に戻ることを'''宮入り'''（みやいり）という。この語は、稚児や[[Ujigami|氏子]]から奉納される山車などが神社から出る・入る場合にも用いられる。 == 渡御行列 == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance|Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum|Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}. In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku|Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''神幸祭'''（しんこうさい）は、神霊の御幸{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}}（ぎょこう、みゆき）が行われる[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の祭礼。'''神幸式'''（しんこうしき）とも言う。多くの場合、神霊が宿った[[Shintai|神体]]や[[Yorishiro|依り代]]などを[[Mikoshi|神輿]]に移して、[[Ujigami|氏子]]地域内に御幸したり、[[Otabisho|御旅所]]や元宮に渡御したりする。神輿や鳳輦の登場する祭礼のほとんどは、神幸祭の一種といえる。 In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == 概要 == 神幸祭の一般的な意義は産土神が氏子の土地を巡る事で神威を示し、神と氏子が親しく交歓する機会にある{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。神事が混交している場合もあるが、神幸祭の形式は神の出自や性格によって次の3つに大別できる{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 ; Miare type A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. ; misogi type : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. Oide type This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. Gism type A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[Mikoshi|mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 御旅所に向う神幸祭の流れは、 # 神輿などに神霊を移す神事 # 神社から御旅所への渡御 # 御旅所での神事や奉納（御旅所祭） # 御旅所から神社への還御 # 神霊を還す神事 In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. 神輿を巡幸のために神社の境内から出すことを'''宮出し'''（みやだし）、巡幸を終えた神輿が境内に戻ることを'''宮入り'''（みやいり）という。この語は、稚児や[[Ujigami|氏子]]から奉納される山車などが神社から出る・入る場合にも用いられる。 == 渡御行列 == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance|Lion dance]], and dances. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. ; misogi type : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == 渡御行列 == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum|Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}. In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku|Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''神幸祭'''（しんこうさい）は、神霊の御幸{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}}（ぎょこう、みゆき）が行われる[[Shinto shrine|神社]]の祭礼。'''神幸式'''（しんこうしき）とも言う。多くの場合、神霊が宿った[[Shintai|神体]]や[[Yorishiro|依り代]]などを[[Mikoshi|神輿]]に移して、[[Ujigami|氏子]]地域内に御幸したり、[[Otabisho|御旅所]]や元宮に渡御したりする。神輿や鳳輦の登場する祭礼のほとんどは、神幸祭の一種といえる。 In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. ; Miare type A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. ; misogi type : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. Oide type This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. Gism type A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[Mikoshi|mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai|Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku|Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

revision.comment: SiliconProphet moved page [[Jingu Sai]] to [[Shinko-shiki]]: mistranslated title
revision.text:
[[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{Refn|御幸とは貴人の外出が幸せをもたらすと信じられたことに基づく言葉で、天皇の御幸を[[行幸]]（ぎょうこう）と言い、神の臨行は神幸と言う『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai|Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance|Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum|Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}. In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku|Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == 出典 == {{Reflist}} == 注釈 == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}}{{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=harv|和書}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} [[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{Refn|The word "mikou" is based on the belief that a nobleman's outing would bring happiness, and the emperor's visit is called [[gyoko]], while the god's visit is called "jinko.『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai|Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro|Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}. === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a malevolent spirit or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance|Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum|Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}. In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku|Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} [[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|杉森八幡社祭礼]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{Refn|The word "mikou" is based on the belief that a nobleman's outing would bring happiness, and the emperor's visit is called [[gyoko]], while the god's visit is called "jinko.『世界大百科事典 第2版』「神幸祭」 （平凡社）。|group="注釈"}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association.{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}} Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity.{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}} === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a [[malevolent spirit]] or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|先導役の猿田彦

面掛行列（御霊神社）]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]]

Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association.{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}} Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity.{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}} === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a [[malevolent spirit]] or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|森田|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|Sarutahiko, the leader of the parade

Menkake Procession (Goryo Shrine)]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=[[森田玲]]|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}
