(According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|Sarutahiko, the leader of the parade

Menkake Procession (Goryo Shrine)]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{Reflist|group=注釈}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=Morita|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|trans-title=Japanese Festivals and Shinto Festivals|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20220207203300|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118}} {{AfC submission|t||ts=20220207130409|u=SiliconProphet|ns=118|demo=}} [[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Sugimori Hachimangu Shrine Festival]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{efn|The word "mikou" is based on the belief that a nobleman's outing would bring happiness, and the emperor's visit is called [[gyoko]], while the god's visit is called "jinko.World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition, "Shinko Matsuri" (Heibonsha)。}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a [[malevolent spirit]] or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|Sarutahiko, the leader of the parade

The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. {{AFC submission|d|v|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=Serial Number 54129|declinets=20220220143140|ts=20220207203300}} {{AFC comment|1=There are other issues too. The prose accepts the existence of genies, etc., in Wikipedia's voice; it reads more as an essay than an encyclopedia entry; and not only does it rely on insufficient sources, it has large amounts (I.e., the majority) of uncited text. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|Sarutahiko, the leader of the parade

Menkake Procession (Goryo Shrine)]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. The procession includes portable shrines, floats, lion dances and dances. The operation of the mikoshi differs for each festival, with some being carried solemnly, some being carried vigorously, and some being carried on boats or in the water. In some cases, these forms are associated with the gods of the festival, and in other cases, they are spur-of-the-moment forms that have become customary. There are two types of floats, one with a role similar to that of a portable shrine and the other as a votive offering, and there are also two types of lion dances and dances, one for rituals and the other for votive offerings. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=Morita|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|trans-title=Japanese Festivals and Shinto Festivals|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

revision.comment: +comment pointing to original source for this EN text -- the JA WP article at [[:ja:神幸祭]]
revision.text:
{{AFC submission|d|v|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=Serial Number 54129|declinets=20220220143140|ts=20220207203300}} {{AFC comment|1=There are other issues too. The prose accepts the existence of genies, etc., in Wikipedia's voice; it reads more as an essay than an encyclopedia entry; and not only does it rely on insufficient sources, it has large amounts (I.e., the majority) of uncited text. [[User talk:Serial Number 54129|SN54129]] 14:31, 20 February 2022 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=Appears to be a machine translation of the article at [[:ja:神幸祭]] (''Shinkō-sai''). ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]'' 20:02, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} [[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Sugimori Hachimangu Shrine Festival]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{efn|The word "mikou" is based on the belief that a nobleman's outing would bring happiness, and the emperor's visit is called [[gyoko]], while the god's visit is called "jinko.World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition, "Shinko Matsuri" (Heibonsha)。}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a [[malevolent spirit]] or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Oide type === This is a type of festival in which a travel agency is set up on the territory of the deity, and the deity is asked to go out to meet with the people. The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == The Procession == [[File:面掛行列（天狗）.JPG|thumb|Sarutahiko, the leader of the parade

Menkake Procession (Goryo Shrine)]] The procession is one of the occasions when many Ujiko can be involved in the festival. They can be the bearers of the portable shrines, or in larger festivals, they can be the procession of floats, [[Lion dance]], and dances. The order of the procession varies depending on the festival, but often there is a [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] at the head of the procession, followed by [[Gohei|Gohin]], Kasaboko, lions, [[Nobori|Banners]], high lanterns, [[Drum]]s, and other items that serve as dew-droppers or indicate the head of the procession. or indicate the head of the group, and the portable shrine often comes.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} In addition, the procession includes [[Kannushi|Priest]], [[Miko|Priestess]], children, floats, lions, and dances. The procession is also musically diverse, with [[Gagaku]] played by the Shinto priests, and festival music and [[Ondō|Ondo]] played by the Ujiko. In addition, during the procession, in Ayazato, Sanriku-cho and Yoshihama, Sanriku-cho, the sudden and inexplicable movement of the portable shrine is called gijimu-mikoshi. The main shrine in the Yoshihama-Nejiro district of Sanriku Town is said to be the Ara Mikoshi. Also, when the gizim is about to occur, the Gongen-sama and the salt purifier will try their best to calm the movement. In addition, when a [[Mikoshi]] moves forward or backward, it is called a gizumu-mikoshi (this seems to be an idea based on the will of the gods). In addition, there are times when the portable shrine moves in a mysterious way during the cruise, and this is called a gizumu mikoshi. Every year, 16 people are chosen to carry the mikoshi, and if they are not available, they are chosen from their relatives. In the Ayazato district, it is called Rikushaku. In Ofunato Town, Mori Town in Ofunato City, Inokawa Town in Ofunato City, Tachine-cho in Ofunato City, Nikoroichi Town in Ofunato City, Ayazato in Ofunato City, Yoshihama in Ofunato City, etc., hand dances are performed in front of the portable shrine. (performed by women and children) This is accompanied by a musical float, and each area has its own unique style. Also, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, there is a cart float with people on the back of a truck beating drums. (Currently, there are no such floats on the road for safety reasons.) The floats are decorated with elaborate "pictures". In Kamaishi City, as in Yoshihama, there will be floats with people on the back of trucks. They are accompanied by a sound system (two in the front and two in the back), making it a big event. In Morimachi, there will also be a float with a pavilion. (Ki-machi, Shimo-machi and Yoshino-machi) will use the Tanabata music of Mori. The performances include the Genpei War, Nasu Yoichi, the Battle of Kawanakajima, and the Osaka Summer War. (Was this thought up in the Meiji era when there were electric wires?) During the nighttime processions, lanterns are added to the floats and the floats are lit up. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=Morita|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|trans-title=Japanese Festivals and Shinto Festivals|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

The Matsuo Matsuri of [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Taisha]] is an example. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=Morita|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|trans-title=Japanese Festivals and Shinto Festivals|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

{{AFC submission|d|v|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=Serial Number 54129|declinets=20220220143140|ts=20220207203300}} {{AFC comment|1=There are other issues too. The prose accepts the existence of genies, etc., in Wikipedia's voice; it reads more as an essay than an encyclopedia entry; and not only does it rely on insufficient sources, it has large amounts (I.e., the majority) of uncited text. [[User talk:Serial Number 54129|SN54129]] 14:31, 20 February 2022 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=Appears to be a machine translation of the article at [[:ja:神幸祭]] (''Shinkō-sai''). ‑‑ [[User:Eirikr|Eiríkr Útlendi]] │''[[User talk:Eirikr|Tala við mig]]'' 20:02, 21 February 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|other}} [[File:Reappear_of_the_daimyo's_procession,_at_Akasaka,_Otowa.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Sugimori Hachimangu Shrine Festival]] '''Shinko-shiki''' is a festival at [[Shinto shrine]]s where a divine spirit{{efn|The word "mikou" is based on the belief that a nobleman's outing would bring happiness, and the emperor's visit is called [[gyoko]], while the god's visit is called "jinko.World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition, "Shinko Matsuri" (Heibonsha)。}} goes on a journey. It is also called '''Shinko-shiki'''. In most cases, the [[Shintai]] or [[Yorishiro]], in which the spirits of the gods reside, are transferred to a [[Mikoshi]], which then travels to the [[Ujigami]] community, or to the [[Otabisho]] or Moto-miya shrine. Most festivals featuring portable shrines and imperial carriages are considered to be a type of Shinko Matsuri. In the broad sense, it refers to the entire go-kosai, while in the narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as an itinerary office. In a narrower sense, it refers to the outward journey from the shrine to the destination, such as a travel agency. In the latter case, the term "kankosai" is used for the outward journey from the destination to the shrine. In a broader sense, the term "Togyo-sai" also refers to the whole process of "Miyuki" (procession). == Overview == The general significance of Shinko-sai is that it is an opportunity for the god of the birthplace to show his divine authority by touring the land of the Ujiko, and for the god and the Ujiko to mingle in close association.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} Although the rituals may be mixed, the form of the Shinkosai can be roughly divided into the following three categories according to the origin and character of the deity.{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}} === Miare type === A form in which a deity is welcomed into a shrine from the land of Miare, where the deity sits or descends, for the purpose of regenerating the deity's authority. Aoi Matsuri]] is an example of this. === misogi type === : A form of misogi in which a haunting god, such as a [[malevolent spirit]] or a plague god, is sent to a misogi site on the waterfront or in the sea to be purified together with the shrine's members and sacred objects. The original form of [[Gion Matsuri]], the Goryokai, was a purification rite. The [[Abare Festival]] in Udezu, for example, is a form of misogi that has become less common today. === Gism type === A phenomenon seen in Ayazato and Yoshihama in Sanriku Town during the parade of portable shrines. The mysterious movement of the mikoshi is called gism mikoshi. When this happens, salt is sprinkled on the ground and the Gongen-sama tries to calm it down. This phenomenon is also thought to be caused by the will of God. (According to a person from the Ayazato district) The Ichikishimahime Shrine, the main shrine of the Nezumi district of Yoshihama, is an Ara-mikoshi. In addition, in Yoshihama, Sanriku Town, [[mikoshi]] is said to be a violent one. The portable shrines of each shrine have four bells attached to the string (front and back together), which make a "jingling" sound when cruising and spin violently. {{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 It is said that originally the divine spirit was welcomed at the [[Altar|Ritual]] in the village, but when the altar developed into a shrine, the act of welcoming remained as a "homecoming" with the opposite meaning, and became a Shinkosai. For this reason, the site of descent, such as a rock formation, became the go-tabisho, and today it is often the former shrine or the original site of the shrine{{Sfn|Morita|2015|pp=41-51}}。 The flow of the Shinto ritual to the Otabisho is as follows. # The ritual of transferring the divine spirit to the portable shrine, etc. # The procession from the shrine to the Otabisho # Rituals and dedications at the Otabisho (Otabisho Festival) # Return from the O-brigyo to the shrine # Rituals to return the spirits of the deities In the process of steps 2 and 4, they often go on a pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. In the process of 2 and 4, they often go on pilgrimage within the Ujiko area. If they do not go to the Otabisho, the spirits go to the Ujiko area to look around or to perform rituals at a certain place. When a portable shrine is taken out of the shrine grounds for a parade, it is called '''Miyadashi''', and when the portable shrine returns to the shrine grounds after the parade, it is called '''Miyairi'''. This word is also used when children or floats dedicated by [[Ujigami|Ujiko]] leave or enter the shrine. == References == {{Reflist}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|last=Morita|title=日本の祭と神賑|date=2015|publisher=創元社|isbn=9784422230351|trans-title=Japanese Festivals and Shinto Festivals|ref=}} [[:Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[:Category:Shinto festivals]] [[:Category:Shinto]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone}} <!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Shinko-shiki]] before draftification: *[[Jingu Sai]] -->[[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] Shinko-shiki (神幸式), also known as Shinko-sai (神幸祭)<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA351&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA143&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA147&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan’s Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA196&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA506&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a shinto ritual of moving the [[shintai]] of a [[kami]] in a procession<ref name=":02">https://archive.ph/UuQJU</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Coogan |first=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref>. The Kami's [[shintai]] will be removed from the main [[Shinto shrine]] and be put in a {{Ill|Mikoshi|en}}, or a movable shrine.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It usually takes place during an annual festival for a shrine<ref name=":12" /> The parade is considered a way for the kami to review and affirm the boundaries of a neighborhood or parish.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
There is high regional variation on how Shinko-shikis work.<ref name=":2" /> The most visible part of the parade is a group of marchers who walk or ride along a set route. The parades require organization and resources, and they are often organized as part of a shrine's ritual or related activities.<ref name=":2" />
This festival can take place on land or water, and the route and places visited are based on tradition. The festival can also include costumes and special rituals. It is sometimes called "O-watari" (お渡り) .<ref name=":02" />
The {{Ill|Gion Matsuri|en}} of {{Ill|Yasaka Shrine|en}} is a famous example of Shinko-sai.<ref name=":02" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko Shiki is specifically associated with the visit of kami to its parishioners, but there is a wide range of performance and interpretation.<ref name=":2" />
For example Tokyo Shinko-shiki is rowdy and has lots of drinking, while in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]] it is quiet and formal.<ref name=":2" />
== References == {{reflist}}
{{Shinto shrines}}

{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} <!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Shinko-shiki]] before draftification: *[[Jingu Sai]] -->[[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] Shinko-shiki (神幸式), also known as Shinko-sai (神幸祭)<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA351&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA143&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA147&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan’s Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA196&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA506&dq=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a shinto ritual of moving the [[shintai]] of a [[kami]] in a procession<ref name=":02">https://archive.ph/UuQJU</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref>.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Coogan |first=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref>. The Kami's [[shintai]] will be removed from the main [[Shinto shrine]] and be put in a {{Ill|Mikoshi|en}}, or a movable shrine.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It usually takes place during an annual festival for a shrine<ref name=":12" /> The parade is considered a way for the kami to review and affirm the boundaries of a neighborhood or parish.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
There is high regional variation on how Shinko-shikis work.<ref name=":2" /> The most visible part of the parade is a group of marchers who walk or ride along a set route. The parades require organization and resources, and they are often organized as part of a shrine's ritual or related activities.<ref name=":2" />
This festival can take place on land or water, and the route and places visited are based on tradition. The festival can also include costumes and special rituals. It is sometimes called "O-watari" (お渡り) .<ref name=":02" />
The {{Ill|Gion Matsuri|en}} of {{Ill|Yasaka Shrine|en}} is a famous example of Shinko-sai.<ref name=":02" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko Shiki is specifically associated with the visit of kami to its parishioners, but there is a wide range of performance and interpretation.<ref name=":2" />
For example Tokyo Shinko-shiki is rowdy and has lots of drinking, while in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]] it is quiet and formal.<ref name=":2" />
== References == {{reflist}}
{{Shinto shrines}}
[[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[simple:Shinko-shiki]] [[ja:神幸祭]]

|title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-sai&q=Shinko-sai&hl=en |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a shinto ritual of moving the [[shintai]] of a [[kami]] in a procession.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Coogan |first=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&dq=Shinko-shiki&q=Shinko-shiki&hl=en |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The Kami's [[shintai]] will be removed from the main [[Shinto shrine]] and be put in a {{Ill|Mikoshi|en}}, or a movable shrine.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It usually takes place during an annual festival for a shrine<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The parade is considered a way for the kami to review and affirm the boundaries of a neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
There is high regional variation on how Shinko-shikis work.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" /> The most visible part of the parade is a group of marchers who walk or ride along a set route. The parades require organization and resources, and they are often organized as part of a shrine's ritual or related activities.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This festival can take place on land or water, and the route and places visited are based on tradition. The festival can also include costumes and special rituals. It is sometimes called "O-watari" (お渡り) .<ref name="https-3951a" />
The {{Ill|Gion Matsuri|en}} of {{Ill|Yasaka Shrine|en}} is a famous example of Shinko-sai.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko Shiki is specifically associated with the visit of kami to its parishioners, but there is a wide range of performance and interpretation.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example Tokyo Shinko-shiki is rowdy and has lots of drinking, while in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]] it is quiet and formal.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The Kami's [[shintai]] will be removed from the main [[Shinto shrine]] and be put in a {{Ill|Mikoshi|en}}, or a movable shrine.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It usually takes place during an annual festival for a shrine<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The parade is considered a way for the kami to review and affirm the boundaries of a neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
There is high regional variation on how Shinko-shikis work.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" /> The most visible part of the parade is a group of marchers who walk or ride along a set route. The parades require organization and resources, and they are often organized as part of a shrine's ritual or related activities.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This festival can take place on land or water, and the route and places visited are based on tradition. The festival can also include costumes and special rituals. It is sometimes called "O-watari" (お渡り) .<ref name="https-3951a" />
The {{Ill|Gion Matsuri|en}} of {{Ill|Yasaka Shrine|en}} is a famous example of Shinko-sai.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko Shiki is specifically associated with the visit of kami to its parishioners, but there is a wide range of performance and interpretation.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example Tokyo Shinko-shiki is rowdy and has lots of drinking, while in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]] it is quiet and formal.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

|date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
