{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

{{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This [[:en:Festival|Festival]] may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in [[:en:Yuzawa, Akita|Yuzawa]], it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Şinko şiki]][[en:Shinko-shiki]][[ja:神幸祭]][[simple:Shinko-shiki]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q123907414]] Wikidata name: shinkō-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Religious ceremony}} {{tone|date=November 2023}} [[File:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|Shinko shiki for {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.]] [[File:Miyamatsuri.JPG|thumb|Underwater shinko shiki at {{ill|Yatsurugi Shrine|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsurugi Shrine|qq=}}]] '''Shinko-shiki''' (神幸式), also known as '''Shinko-sai''' (神幸祭),<ref>{{Cite book |last=Sanmi |first=Sasaki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MorTAgAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA351 |title=Chado the Way of Tea: A Japanese Tea Master's Almanac |date=2011-10-18 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0036-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Plutschow |first=Herbert |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7X_7AQAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA143 |title=Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan: With a Selection from P.G. O'Neill's Photographic Archive of Matsuri |date=2013-11-05 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-24698-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA147 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=McVeigh |first=Brian J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HvUABAAAQBAJ&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA196 |title=Interpreting Japan: Approaches and Applications for the Classroom |date=2014-07-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-91304-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DTPJpanTizwC&dq=Shinko-sai&pg=PA506 |title=Changing World Religions, Cults & Occult |publisher=Jerry Stokes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Japan |first=Asiatic Society of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xYU0AAAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan |date=1935 |publisher=The Society |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvopAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-sai |title=The New Encyclopaedia Britannica: Macropaedia : Knowledge in depth |date=1998 |publisher=Encyclopaedia Britannica |isbn=978-0-85229-663-9 |language=en}}</ref> is a ceremonial practice within [[Shinto|Shintoism]] involving the procession of a [[kami]]'s [[shintai]], or divine object.<ref name="https-3951a">https://archive.today/20230411204108/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/bts/detail/?id=3951</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hirayama |first=Miyuki |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KkIeAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Matsuri in Shimomura: Experience, Performance, and History in a Japanese Festival |date=2001 |publisher=Indiana University |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=glIJAQAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Basic Terms of Shinto |date=1958 |publisher=(The Association of Shinto Shriners) Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Littleton |first=C. Scott |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=573YAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Shinto: Origins, Rituals, Festivals, Spirits, Sacred Places |date=2002 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-521886-2 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Coogan |first1=Michael David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=888kAQAAIAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |last2=Narayanan |first2=Vasudha |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522190-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qFHVAAAAMAAJ&q=Shinko-shiki |title=Eastern Horizon |date=1964 |publisher=Eastern Horizon Press |language=en}}</ref> The shintai of the ''Kami'' is transferred from the primary Shinto {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} to a [[Mikoshi]], a portable shrine, as part of the ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Shinkō-sai {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinko-sai |access-date=2023-04-11 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> Typically, this ceremony occurs within the context of an annual festival hosted by a shrine.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> The procession is regarded as a means through which the ''Kami'' may inspect and validate the boundaries of a particular neighborhood or parish.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a">{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RLCPn9QV4_UC&dq=shinko-shiki&pg=PA50 |title=Matsuri: Festivals of a Japanese Town |date=1993-03-01 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-1421-2 |language=en}}</ref>
The prominent feature of the procession involves a group of participants who proceed either on foot or through various modes of transportation along a predetermined route. These parades necessitate organizational efforts and resources, commonly arranged as integral components of a shrine's ceremonial or associated endeavors.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
This {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival}} may occur on both land and water, with the route and destinations typically adhering to established traditions. Costumes and specific rituals often feature as part of the festivities. Occasionally referred to as "O-watari," (お渡り) the festival encompasses a range of ceremonial practices and observances.<ref name="https-3951a" />
The [[Gion Matsuri]] held at [[Yasaka Shrine]] serves as a well-known illustration of the Shinko-sai ritual.<ref name="https-3951a" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Root of the Gion Matsuri: The Shinko Matsuri – EBISU恵比壽日藥直送 |url=https://ebisujapan.com/en/blogs/%E6%83%A0%E6%AF%94%E5%A3%BDblog/%E7%A5%87%E5%9C%92%E7%A5%AD%E7%9A%84%E6%A0%B9%E6%9C%AC-%E7%A5%9E%E5%B9%B8%E7%A5%AD |access-date=2023-05-21 |website=ebisujapan.com}}</ref>
Shinko-shiki is primarily linked with the ''Kami's'' visitation to its adherents, although interpretations and ceremonial practices can vary significantly.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
For example, the {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} Shinko-shiki tends to be lively with a focus on revelry and alcohol consumption, whereas in {{ill|Yuzawa, Akita|en|Yuzawa, Akita|WD=Q633272|lt=Yuzawa}}, it is characterized by a more subdued and formal atmosphere.<ref name="ashkenazi-1993a" />
== References == {{reflist}}
{{Shinto shrines}}
